<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]], reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]]. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]]. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
{{translated page|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|version=103916794|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]]
[[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]], reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]]. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]]. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
{{translated page|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|version=103916794|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]], reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]]. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]]. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
{{translated page|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|version=103916794|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]], reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]]. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]]. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
{{translated page|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|version=103916794|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]], reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]]. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]]. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
{{translated page|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|version=103916794|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]], reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]]. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]]. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
{{translated page|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|version=103916794|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the [[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]], reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]]. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from [[:en:Kyushu|Kyūshū]] to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]]. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
{{translated page|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|version=103916794|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]]

Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code}}, reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}} to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|WD=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako}} increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyūshū}} to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin}}. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11410913]] Wikidata name: koseki system in classical Japan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} in the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the {{ill|Suō Province|en|Suō Province|WD=Q1053037|lt=Suō Province}} capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code}}, reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}} to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|WD=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako}} increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyūshū}} to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin}}. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}

This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}} to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|WD=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako}} increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyūshū}} to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin}}. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سیستم ثبت خانواده در ژاپن باستان]][[ja:古代日本の戸籍制度]]
'''Ancient Japanese Census Registration System''' (古代日本の戸籍制度, Kodai Nihon no Koseki Seido) refers to the census registration system established during the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} in the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} for population management. Notable examples include the {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (670 AD) and the {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} (690 AD). Some portions of these ancient census records survive as part of the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, while other records have been uncovered as {{ill|lacquered paper documents|ja|漆紙文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lacquered paper documents|1=urushigami monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565404}} at sites such as the {{ill|Akita Castle|ja|秋田城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田城|1=Akita Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595283}} and {{ill|Tagajō|ja|多賀城跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagajō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多賀城|1=Taga Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q870867}}, as well as in the form of wooden tablets (木簡) in provincial locations like the {{ill|Suō Province|en|Suō Province|WD=Q1053037|lt=Suō Province}} capital. Advances in infrared analysis have facilitated the deciphering of these records. The Kōgo Census, compiled in 670 AD (the 9th year of Emperor Tenji's reign), marked Japan's first nationwide census. Although census records were typically destroyed after 30 years, the Kōgo Census was designated for permanent preservation. This census coincided with the enforcement of the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code}}, reflecting its legal framework. == The Beginnings of Census Records == Early mentions of census compilation appear in: * '''540 AD (Emperor Kinmei's reign):''' "Foreigners such as Qin and Han people, settled in the regions and listed in census registers." This indicates that census registration initially focused on managing immigrant populations.<ref>{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}</ref> * '''569 AD:''' Emperor Kinmei ordered inspections of tax registries in the {{ill|Shirai no Miyake|ja|白猪屯倉|1=Shirai no Miyake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Miyake|qq=}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}} to ensure proper registration of taxable individuals. * '''574 AD:''' {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|WD=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako}} increased census efforts in Kibi, refining population control in granary-associated labor. These efforts primarily targeted immigrants and administrative groups such as granary laborers, differing from the comprehensive population control established under the Ritsuryō system. == Kōgo Census == The {{ill|Kōgo Census|ja|庚午年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgo Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}} was compiled in 670 AD to implement the reforms proposed by the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}. It represented Japan's first comprehensive national census, covering areas from {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyūshū}} to {{ill|Kantō|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. However, the original records are no longer extant, and questions remain about whether all societal classes were included. Subsequent records and lawsuits involving surname changes or social status disputes, however, demonstrate its use as a foundational document. The Kōgo Census was copied and stored in the central government archives until the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. == Kōin Census == The {{ill|Kōin Census|ja|庚寅年籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Census|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古代日本の戸籍制度|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, compiled in 690 AD under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, represented the next stage in census development. It detailed all households and individuals, enabling the implementation of the {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} and comprehensive tax collection. == Census Records and Reused Documents == Due to the high value of paper in ancient Japan, discarded census records were often reused by temples and government offices for other purposes. Many such reused documents, now known as {{ill|Recycled Paper Documents|ja|紙背文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Recycled Paper Documents|1=shihai monjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605537}}, have been preserved in collections such as the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin}}. == Conclusion == The comprehensive compilation of census records continued until the mid-10th century but declined as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} weakened. The establishment of the {{ill|Shōen system|ja|荘園制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荘園|1=manorialism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1550557}} reduced the need for centralized population management, marking the end of systematic census compilation. == Related Chronology == - 540 AD: Census registration of immigrant groups. - 670 AD: Compilation of the Kōgo Census. - 690 AD: Compilation of the Kōin Census under the Asuka Kiyomihara Code. - 702 AD: The oldest surviving census, the {{ill|Hanpurī Census|ja|半布里戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpurī Census|1=Hanpurī Census|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407005}}, was compiled. == References == <references/> == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Atsushi Nito |title=Research on Shōsōin Documents |journal=National Museum of Japanese History Bulletin |date=2014-12 |volume=192 |pages=57–64 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00002189|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju System|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju System|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * {{ill|Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|ja|古代日本の地方官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japanese Provincial Administration|1=local government in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410912}} * {{ill|Census Records|ja|計帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Census Records|1=Census Records|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631032}}
